phase1.output:
Enter your name: Alice
Enter your age: 20
Enter your GPA: 3.75

=== Student Info ===
Name: Alice
Age: 20
GPA: 3.75
phase1.explanation:
Scanner is Java's built-in way to read user input:

Import: import java.util.Scanner;

Create Scanner: Scanner scanner = new Scanner(System.in);

Reading methods:
• nextLine() — reads a full line of text
• nextInt() — reads an integer
• nextDouble() — reads a decimal number
• next() — reads a single word
• nextBoolean() — reads true/false

Output methods:
• System.out.print() — prints without newline
• System.out.println() — prints with newline
• System.out.printf() — formatted printing (like C's printf)

Pro tip: ALWAYS close the Scanner with scanner.close() to free resources!

⚠️ Gotcha: nextInt() leaves a newline in the buffer — if you use nextLine() after nextInt(), it'll read an empty string! Use scanner.nextLine() after nextInt() to clear the buffer.

phase2.errorMessage:
Enter your age: 25
Enter your name: Name: , Age: 25

(No error message — but the program SKIPS the name input!)
phase2.explanation:
This is a subtle bug that doesn't crash, but breaks the program!

After nextInt(), the newline character is left in the buffer. nextLine() immediately reads that leftover newline and returns an empty string.

The fix: add an extra scanner.nextLine() after nextInt() to clear the buffer:

int age = scanner.nextInt();
scanner.nextLine();  // ← clears buffer
String name = scanner.nextLine();

This is why order matters with Scanner methods!

phase3.prompt:
Write a program that:
1. Asks for a user's favorite color (String)
2. Asks for their lucky number (int)
3. Prints: "Your favorite color is [color] and your lucky number is [number]"

Handle the nextInt buffer issue correctly!
dialogueHints: Don't forget to handle the newline after nextInt() with an extra nextLine()
Store the color in a String and the number in an int
Print both values in one println statement.
phase3.openingDialogue: Now let's make your program actually TALK to the user~ 💬
